heroOverview: Hypopyon is a collection of white blood cells that settles in a visible horizontal layer at the bottom of the anterior chamber of the eye, caused by severe intraocular inflammation or infection. Bilateral hypopyon — simultaneous accumulation in both eyes — occurs in fewer than 10% of hypopyon cases and almost always signals a systemic or endogenous cause rather than a local eye problem. The most common underlying causes are bacterial endophthalmitis seeded through the bloodstream, severe uveitis associated with inflammatory diseases such as Behçet disease, or fungal infection spreading to both eyes in immunocompromised patients. Prompt identification of the cause is critical: bilateral hypopyon is an ophthalmic emergency, and delays in treatment can lead to permanent vision loss or blindness in one or both eyes. Treatment must address both the eye inflammation and the underlying systemic condition simultaneously.

keyStats.prevalence: Hypopyon complicates approximately 3-10% of cases of anterior uveitis; bilateral occurrence is uncommon and strongly suggests systemic disease or endogenous endophthalmitis
keyStats.demographics: Affects all ages; endogenous endophthalmitis peaks in immunocompromised adults; Behçet-associated hypopyon is most common in young adults aged 20-45 from the Middle East, East Asia, and Mediterranean regions
keyStats.avgAge: Behçet disease presents most often between ages 20 and 45; fungal endophthalmitis peaks in immunocompromised adults of any age
keyStats.globalCases: Behçet disease affects an estimated 1 in 10,000 to 1 in 370 people depending on geographic region; endogenous endophthalmitis accounts for 2-8% of all endophthalmitis cases

definition: Hypopyon (ICD-10: H20.053, bilateral) is the accumulation of a hypopyon — an exudate of polymorphonuclear leucocytes, fibrin, and inflammatory debris — in the inferior angle of the anterior chamber, visible as a white or cream-coloured fluid level below the cornea. In bilateral hypopyon, this inflammatory exudate appears simultaneously in both eyes. The anterior chamber is the fluid-filled space between the cornea and the iris, and the gravitational settling of dense white cells creates the characteristic layering visible to the naked eye or by slit-lamp examination. Hypopyon does not directly damage the cornea in mild cases, but the underlying cause — usually severe iridocyclitis, bacterial or fungal endophthalmitis, or corneal ulceration with secondary spread — can destroy intraocular structures rapidly. Bilateral involvement narrows the differential diagnosis substantially; unilateral hypopyon is commonly caused by corneal ulcer, contact-lens-related keratitis, or postoperative endophthalmitis, whereas bilateral disease requires a search for systemic vasculitis (especially Behçet disease), haematogenous infection (Candida, Aspergillus, or bacteria in a septicaemic patient), drug toxicity, or intraocular lymphoma masquerading as uveitis. The condition falls under the specialist domain of ophthalmology, often in collaboration with infectious disease, rheumatology, or internal medicine.

typesClassification.description: Caused by bacteria or fungi reaching both eyes through the bloodstream in a septicaemic patient, most commonly Candida albicans in patients with prolonged intravenous lines or immunosuppression, or bacterial organisms such as Klebsiella in diabetic patients with liver abscess.

The classic form of bilateral recurrent hypopyon, driven by HLA-B51-associated occlusive vasculitis of retinal and uveal vessels; episodes recur abruptly, last days, and can lead to cumulative vision loss with each attack if not suppressed.

Severe bilateral microbial keratitis, seen in bilateral contact-lens wearers or after chemical splash injury, can generate enough anterior chamber inflammation to produce a hypopyon in each eye independently.
Certain intravitreal injections, topical medications with preservatives in high doses, or systemic drug toxicity can provoke sterile bilateral anterior chamber inflammation, though this is rare.

Large B-cell lymphoma within the vitreous and anterior chamber can mimic bilateral uveitis with hypopyon in older adults, requiring aqueous and vitreous sampling for cytology to distinguish it from inflammatory disease.

primarySymptoms: Visible white or cream-coloured fluid level at the bottom of one or both eyes, often the presenting sign noticed by the patient or a companion in the mirror.
Severe photophobia — intense pain or discomfort with any light exposure, caused by ciliary muscle spasm from anterior chamber inflammation.
Marked reduction in visual acuity in one or both eyes, which can range from mild blurring to light-perception only depending on the extent of the hypopyon and vitreous involvement.
Deep, aching ocular pain that worsens with eye movement and is often described as a pressure sensation from within the eye.
Intense redness of the eye (ciliary flush), a deep violaceous injection around the limbus that differs from the superficial redness of conjunctivitis.
Excessive tearing (epiphora) without discharge, driven by irritation of the anterior segment.
Eyelid swelling (periorbital oedema) in moderate to severe cases, particularly with endogenous bacterial endophthalmitis.
Floaters or a hazy, cloudy visual field when inflammation extends posteriorly to involve the vitreous.
Headache and fever when the hypopyon is caused by systemic infection or Behçet disease flare.
Oral ulcers and genital ulcers in patients with Behçet disease, occurring simultaneously or just before ocular involvement.
earlyWarningSigns: Sudden onset of photophobia more intense than usual in a patient with known uveitis or Behçet disease.
A white or yellowish tinge at the bottom of the cornea noticed in the mirror, even without pain.
Rapid decrease in visual clarity over hours rather than days in a patient who is immunocompromised.
New-onset eye redness with headache and fever in a patient with an indwelling central venous catheter or known fungaemia.
emergencySigns: Complete loss of light perception or hand-motion vision in either eye — requires same-day vitreous tap and intravitreal antibiotics.
Bilateral hypopyon in any immunocompromised or septic patient, which is an ophthalmic emergency.
Rapidly rising intraocular pressure (>30 mmHg) with hypopyon, indicating angle blockage requiring urgent intervention.
Signs of Behçet-associated retinal vasculitis with visual field loss, where delay in immunosuppression risks irreversible damage.
Corneal perforation or imminent melting — surgical emergency requiring urgent tectonic graft.

causes.description: Bacteria from a distant focus of infection — endocarditis, liver abscess, urinary sepsis — seed the choroidal circulation and penetrate the blood–ocular barrier, causing explosive intraocular inflammation with pus formation in the anterior chamber; bilateral seeding occurs when bacteraemia is sustained or high-grade.

Fungal elements circulating in the blood of patients receiving prolonged IV antibiotics, total parenteral nutrition, or immunosuppressants seed the retinal and choroidal vessels; Candida endophthalmitis can produce bilateral creamy vitreous balls and anterior hypopyon in severely immunocompromised patients.

This HLA-B51-linked systemic vasculitis triggers sudden-onset, recurrent, nongranulomatous panuveitis with anterior chamber hypopyon; the mechanism involves T-lymphocyte-mediated occlusive vasculitis of small uveal and retinal vessels, producing ischaemia and a vigorous neutrophilic exudate.

Corneal ulcers, most often from Pseudomonas aeruginosa in contact-lens wearers or Acanthamoeba in swimmers, can extend inflammation into the anterior chamber via aqueous toxins and direct breach of Descemet membrane, forming a hypopyon secondary to the keratitis rather than to an independent uveitis.

Bilateral simultaneous intraocular surgery or lens implantation with toxic anterior segment syndrome can produce sterile hypopyon in both eyes due to introduction of pyrogens, irrigating solution contaminants, or ophthalmic viscosurgical device remnants into both anterior chambers at the same operative session.

Vitreoretinal large B-cell lymphoma releases malignant cells and cytokines that can produce bilateral anterior chamber flare and white-cell layering indistinguishable from sterile uveitis; this diagnosis must be excluded in adults over 50 with bilateral uveitis unresponsive to corticosteroids.

riskFactors.description: Impaired innate and adaptive immunity permits haematogenous seeding of the eye by fungi and bacteria that would ordinarily be cleared, making endogenous bilateral endophthalmitis 3-5 times more common in immunocompromised individuals.
Central lines are the single leading risk factor for Candida bloodstream infection; the longer a line remains in place, the higher the daily probability of fungaemia and subsequent ocular seeding.

Hyperglycaemia impairs neutrophil chemotaxis and promotes Klebsiella liver abscess with secondary endogenous endophthalmitis, a well-documented cause of bilateral hypopyon particularly in East Asian diabetic patients.

HLA-B51 is present in 50-80% of Behçet patients compared with 10-20% of the general population; individuals from the historic Silk Road corridor — Turkey, Iran, Japan, Korea — carry the highest disease prevalence and ocular involvement rates.

Sleeping in contact lenses reduces corneal oxygenation and damages the epithelial barrier, raising the risk of microbial keratitis with secondary hypopyon by roughly 10-15 times compared with daily-wear users.
Non-sterile injection introduces oral flora and skin organisms directly into the bloodstream; Candida and Streptococcus species are both capable of bilateral ocular seeding in this population.
Each prior attack of Behçet-associated hypopyon uveitis predicts future recurrences; eyes that have sustained multiple attacks have progressively less reserve visual function.

affectedDemographics: Young adults aged 20-45 along the Silk Road corridor (Turkey, Middle East, East Asia) with Behçet disease
Immunocompromised adults of any age — transplant recipients, patients on cancer chemotherapy, or those with AIDS
Hospitalised patients with prolonged central venous catheters or on total parenteral nutrition
Diabetic adults, particularly in East Asia, due to Klebsiella liver abscess with ocular seeding
Bilateral contact-lens wearers who sleep in lenses
Adults over 50 with bilateral uveitis refractory to steroids, in whom intraocular lymphoma must be excluded
diagnosisOverview: The diagnosis of hypopyon is clinical — the white fluid level in the anterior chamber is visible on inspection and confirmed by slit-lamp biomicroscopy, which allows the clinician to measure the height of the hypopyon, assess the degree of anterior chamber flare and cells, and examine the iris, lens, and posterior segment. Bilateral involvement immediately narrows the differential toward systemic causes. After examining both eyes, the ophthalmologist pursues a directed systemic workup. Blood cultures, complete blood count, C-reactive protein, and serum (1,3)-β-d-glucan are drawn when infection is suspected. Imaging — CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis — looks for a primary infective source such as endocarditis or liver abscess. When Behçet disease is suspected, the ICBD criteria (International Study Group criteria) are applied, incorporating oral ulcers, genital ulcers, skin lesions, and pathergy test results. In refractory or atypical cases, diagnostic anterior chamber paracentesis and vitreous biopsy are performed; the aqueous specimen is sent for cultures, PCR for viral and bacterial targets, cytology (to exclude lymphoma), and IL-10/IL-6 ratio (elevated in intraocular lymphoma). Intraocular pressure is measured at each visit as hypopyon can obstruct trabecular outflow. The key distinction between infectious and non-infectious hypopyon drives immediate treatment decisions: infectious hypopyon requires intravitreal or systemic antimicrobials, whereas inflammatory hypopyon from Behçet disease requires aggressive immunosuppression.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the hypopyon, quantifies anterior chamber inflammation, and evaluates for corneal involvement, keratic precipitates, and posterior segment extension.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You rest your chin on a padded support while the ophthalmologist examines both eyes using a narrow beam of light and a microscope; the examination takes 5-10 minutes and may include dilation drops for the posterior pole.

diagnosticTests.test: Blood cultures and septic screen (CBC, CRP, blood glucose, (1,3)-β-d-glucan)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies haematogenous infection (bacteria or fungi) as the source of endogenous endophthalmitis, and assesses the severity of the systemic inflammatory response.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood is drawn from a vein; blood cultures require two sets from different sites; the (1,3)-β-d-glucan assay is a single tube sent to a specialist laboratory.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects the primary source of bacteraemia or fungaemia — endocarditis vegetation, liver abscess, lung abscess, or other collection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie in the CT scanner for about 10-15 minutes; intravenous contrast is injected via a cannula; results are available within hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Obtains intraocular fluid for culture, PCR, cytology, and IL-10/IL-6 ratio to distinguish infection from inflammation and exclude lymphoma.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A very fine needle is passed through the corneal limbus under local anaesthetic drops to withdraw about 0.1-0.2 mL of fluid; the procedure takes under five minutes and is well tolerated.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects raised pressure from hypopyon obstructing outflow or secondary glaucoma from prolonged inflammation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A puff of air or a probe gently touches the anaesthetised corneal surface; results are immediate.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates retinal vasculitis and macular involvement in Behçet disease, guiding the urgency and intensity of immunosuppression.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fluorescent dye is injected into an arm vein and photographs of the retina are taken in rapid sequence; OCT uses non-contact laser light scanning of retinal layers — both are painless.

diagnosticTests.test: Pathergy test and ICBD criteria assessment
diagnosticTests.purpose: Supports a diagnosis of Behçet disease when systemic features are present alongside the bilateral hypopyon.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A sterile needle is pricked into the forearm; a raised red papule forming after 24-48 hours constitutes a positive test; the result is combined with clinical features for scoring.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of bilateral hypopyon is directed at both the acute intraocular emergency and the underlying systemic cause. When endogenous infectious endophthalmitis is suspected or confirmed, intravitreal antibiotics (most commonly vancomycin 1 mg/0.1 mL plus ceftazidime 2.25 mg/0.1 mL) are injected directly into the vitreous cavity of each affected eye, often combined with systemic intravenous antibiotics or antifungals (voriconazole or liposomal amphotericin B for fungal disease). Pars plana vitrectomy may be required for severe cases, particularly when vitreous opacity is dense or when the source is fungal. For Behçet-associated bilateral hypopyon, treatment follows rheumatology and ophthalmology joint guidelines: acute attacks are suppressed with high-dose oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day, followed immediately by a disease-modifying agent — azathioprine, cyclosporin A, or increasingly infliximab or interferon-alpha for sight-threatening disease. Topical corticosteroids and cycloplegics (atropine or cyclopentolate) are added in both settings to reduce anterior chamber inflammation and prevent synechiae formation. Sterile post-surgical hypopyon from toxic anterior segment syndrome resolves with intensive topical steroids alone. Raised intraocular pressure is managed with aqueous suppressants; pilocarpine is avoided in active uveitis. Systemic disease control — treating the source abscess, removing the central line, or suppressing the Behçet vasculitis — is as important as local ocular therapy. Serial slit-lamp monitoring, sometimes daily during acute attacks, tracks resolution of the hypopyon and guides step-down of treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravitreal antibiotics (vancomycin + ceftazidime)
medicalTreatments.description: Direct injection into the vitreous cavity bypasses the blood–ocular barrier and achieves antimicrobial concentrations unattainable by systemic dosing; covers Gram-positive organisms (including MRSA) and Gram-negative organisms including Pseudomonas; injected into both eyes when both are affected.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Vision is preserved in approximately 50-70% of eyes treated promptly with intravitreal antibiotics for endogenous bacterial endophthalmitis; outcomes worsen significantly with each 24-hour delay.

medicalTreatments.name: Systemic antifungal therapy (voriconazole or liposomal amphotericin B)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral or intravenous voriconazole is the preferred agent for Candida and Aspergillus endophthalmitis because it achieves measurable intraocular levels; liposomal amphotericin B is used when voriconazole resistance is suspected or tolerance is poor.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates for Candida endophthalmitis with early voriconazole plus intravitreal therapy approach 70-80% in recent series; Aspergillus endophthalmitis has a much poorer prognosis with eye salvage in fewer than 30% of cases.

medicalTreatments.name: High-dose systemic corticosteroids (prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: The first-line acute treatment for Behçet-associated panuveitis; rapidly suppresses the vigorous neutrophilic anterior chamber exudate but must be combined with a steroid-sparing agent to prevent cumulative toxicity and relapse.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolves acute hypopyon in 3-7 days in approximately 80-90% of Behçet flares; corticosteroids alone do not prevent recurrence.

medicalTreatments.name: Azathioprine 2.5 mg/kg/day
medicalTreatments.description: The European League Against Rheumatism (EULAR) guideline recommends azathioprine as first-line maintenance therapy for Behçet uveitis; it reduces attack frequency and corticosteroid requirements.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces rate of further attacks by approximately 50% compared with no immunosuppression in randomised controlled trials of Behçet uveitis.

medicalTreatments.name: Infliximab (anti-TNF biologic)
medicalTreatments.description: Recommended by EULAR for severe or sight-threatening Behçet uveitis refractory to azathioprine; infliximab 5 mg/kg IV infusions every 6-8 weeks rapidly suppress retinal vasculitis and anterior inflammation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Studies report complete remission of Behçet uveitis in 60-80% of patients at 12 months; vision stabilises or improves in the majority of treated eyes.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical corticosteroids and cycloplegics
medicalTreatments.description: Prednisolone acetate 1% drops hourly initially, combined with atropine 1% or cyclopentolate 1%, reduce anterior chamber cellular activity, relieve ciliary spasm, and prevent posterior synechiae formation in both infectious and inflammatory hypopyon.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Topical therapy provides measurable reduction in anterior chamber flare and cells within 24-48 hours and reduces synechiae risk by approximately 60-70% when started promptly.

surgicalOptions.name: Pars plana vitrectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical removal of the vitreous gel when it is densely opaque with inflammatory cells or fungal elements; allows direct sampling for culture and dramatically reduces the fungal or bacterial load; performed under general or local anaesthesia with a small-incision 3-port approach.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Vitrectomy combined with intravitreal antifungals achieves visual recovery to 20/400 or better in approximately 55-65% of fungal endophthalmitis eyes treated within 24-48 hours of presentation; outcomes are substantially better than medical therapy alone in severe cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Anterior chamber washout (paracentesis and irrigation)
surgicalOptions.description: In cases where a dense hypopyon is obstructing the angle and raising pressure acutely, irrigation of the anterior chamber can mechanically reduce the load of white cells and fibrin while awaiting definitive treatment.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Used as a temporising measure; no published cure-rate data as a standalone procedure; typically combined with intravitreal therapy.

alternativeTreatments.name: Immunomodulatory therapy (interferon-alpha 2a)
alternativeTreatments.description: An alternative to anti-TNF biologics for Behçet uveitis that is refractory to conventional immunosuppressants; some case series report complete remission in 70-80% of patients, though it is not universally available.

alternativeTreatments.name: Cyclosporin A
alternativeTreatments.description: An option for Behçet uveitis involving the posterior segment; effective at reducing attack frequency but carries risks of nephrotoxicity and hypertension requiring monitoring.

whySeeSpecialist: Bilateral hypopyon is not a condition to manage in primary care or general emergency medicine. Ophthalmologists with training in uveitis and medical retina are needed to direct the ocular investigation and inject intravitreal medications safely. Concurrent evaluation by an infectious disease specialist is essential when endogenous infection is suspected, and a rheumatologist experienced in Behçet disease should co-manage patients with that diagnosis. Delays of even 12-24 hours in obtaining the correct diagnosis and starting appropriate treatment can result in permanent bilateral visual impairment.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an ophthalmologist who subspecialises in uveitis and medical retina, ideally affiliated with a tertiary centre where same-day vitreous tap, intravitreal injection, and vitrectomy capabilities are available. For Behçet disease, look for a joint uveitis clinic with rheumatology involvement and access to biologic therapy. Ask about the centre's experience with endogenous endophthalmitis and whether an on-call vitreoretinal surgeon is available after hours.
preventionStrategies: In hospitalised patients, remove central venous catheters as soon as they are no longer clinically required to minimise the window for fungal bloodstream infection.
Patients with Behçet disease should maintain regular follow-up with both ophthalmology and rheumatology to sustain disease suppression and detect early relapse.
Contact-lens wearers should never sleep in lenses, should replace lenses on schedule, and should use sterile saline for rinsing cases.
Diabetic patients with fever and flank pain or right upper quadrant pain should have an ophthalmologic examination to screen for haematogenous ocular seeding.
Patients receiving immunosuppressants or chemotherapy should have fundal screening if they develop fever of unknown origin lasting more than 48 hours.
lifestyleModifications: Patients with Behçet disease should avoid triggers that provoke flares, including physical trauma to mucous membranes and psychological stress where feasible.
Strict adherence to prescribed immunosuppressive regimens prevents recurrent attacks; missing doses is a common cause of relapse.
Patients with bilateral visual impairment from recurrent hypopyon attacks should be referred for low vision rehabilitation services early.
Smoking cessation is important as smoking is associated with higher Behçet disease activity in some cohorts.
Patients using intravitreal therapy should keep scheduled injection appointments even when symptom-free, as silent disease activity can damage vision between appointments.

recommended: A balanced anti-inflammatory diet rich in vegetables, fruit, whole grains, and omega-3 fatty acids may support overall immune regulation in Behçet disease
Adequate hydration to support kidney function in patients on cyclosporin A or systemic antifungals
Calcium and vitamin D supplementation for patients on long-term systemic corticosteroids to reduce osteoporosis risk
avoid: Alcohol should be minimised during systemic azathioprine or methotrexate therapy due to hepatotoxicity risk
Grapefruit and grapefruit juice interfere with cyclosporin A metabolism and should be avoided

exerciseGuidelines: There are no specific exercise restrictions for bilateral hypopyon once the acute phase has settled, except avoidance of contact sports or activities risking ocular trauma in patients with compromised corneal or scleral integrity after vitrectomy. Patients recovering from systemic infection should resume activity gradually under medical guidance. Regular low-impact exercise is encouraged for general health and corticosteroid-related weight management.
dailyManagement: Instil topical corticosteroid drops exactly as prescribed — frequency must not be self-reduced early, as premature taper leads to synechiae and pressure spikes.
Monitor and record visual acuity in each eye daily during an acute attack; any sudden drop should prompt same-day contact with the ophthalmologist.
Keep a log of Behçet disease symptoms — oral ulcers, skin lesions, joint pain — to identify early systemic flares that may precede ocular relapse.
Report any new eye redness, photophobia, or visual blurring to the eye unit immediately, even between scheduled appointments.
In patients with central venous catheters, report fever, chills, or any eye symptoms to the treating team immediately for urgent fundal examination.
prognosis: The prognosis of bilateral hypopyon depends almost entirely on the cause and the speed of appropriate treatment. In Behçet disease, each uncontrolled attack reduces visual acuity; without long-term immunosuppression, up to 25% of eyes become legally blind within 10 years of disease onset. With modern anti-TNF biologic therapy, the frequency and severity of attacks can be dramatically reduced, and vision is preserved in the majority of patients who start biologics early. Endogenous bacterial endophthalmitis carries a guarded prognosis — meaningful visual recovery occurs in roughly 50-70% of eyes when treatment is initiated within 24 hours, but delays beyond 48 hours raise the probability of no light perception. Fungal endophthalmitis, particularly Aspergillus, has a much worse outcome. Sterile toxic anterior segment syndrome after bilateral surgery typically resolves completely without long-term vision loss when treated promptly with corticosteroids.
recoveryTimeline: For Behçet hypopyon uveitis treated with corticosteroids, the anterior chamber typically clears within 3-7 days. The inflammation may fully resolve within 2-4 weeks, though systemic maintenance therapy continues indefinitely. For endogenous endophthalmitis, improvement after intravitreal injection is expected within 24-72 hours if the pathogen is sensitive; repeated injections every 48 hours may be needed. Full visual rehabilitation after severe endophthalmitis, including vitrectomy, can take 3-6 months, and some degree of permanent vision loss is common in severe cases.
complications: Posterior synechiae — adhesions between the iris and anterior lens capsule that distort the pupil and can cause acute angle-closure glaucoma if 360-degree adhesion (iris bombé) forms.
Secondary glaucoma — raised intraocular pressure from trabecular blockage by inflammatory debris or synechiae, which if untreated leads to optic nerve damage.
Cataract — prolonged intraocular inflammation and corticosteroid use both promote lens opacity, often requiring surgical extraction.
Cystoid macular oedema — accumulation of fluid in the central retina, the most common cause of permanent vision loss in recurrent Behçet uveitis even when anterior chamber inflammation resolves.
Retinal vascular occlusion — Behçet-associated vasculitis can cause branch or central retinal vein or artery occlusion, resulting in permanent scotomata.
Phthisis bulbi — the end-stage of severely inflamed or infected eyes in which the globe becomes soft, shrunken, and blind following irreversible damage.

supportResources.name: Behçet's UK — Patient Support
supportResources.description: UK-based charity providing information and peer support for people living with Behçet disease.

sources.title: Nussenblatt RB, Whitcup SM. Uveitis: Fundamentals and Clinical Practice, 4th edition. Overview of anterior segment inflammation including hypopyon.
Hatemi G et al. EULAR recommendations for the management of Behçet syndrome. Ann Rheum Dis. 2018;77(6):808-818.
Callegan MC et al. Bacterial endophthalmitis: epidemiology, therapeutics, and bacterium–host interactions. Clin Microbiol Rev. 2002;15(1):111-124.
Lingappan A et al. Exogenous fungal endophthalmitis: risk factors, clinical features, and treatment outcomes. Ophthalmology. 2012;119(1):176-182.

confusedWithConditions.name: Panuveitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Panuveitis involves all three uveal layers including the vitreous and retina, whereas hypopyon specifically refers to the white-cell sediment in the anterior chamber; hypopyon can be a feature of panuveitis but the two diagnoses are not interchangeable.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute glaucoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute angle-closure glaucoma causes a red, painful eye with reduced vision and a mid-dilated pupil, but does not produce a white fluid level; hypopyon must be distinguished from pseudo-hypopyon from pigment or blood settling in the anterior chamber.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cataracts
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cataracts cause gradual painless vision loss without redness or a white fluid level in the anterior chamber; in contrast, bilateral hypopyon presents acutely with inflammation and visible layered exudate.

relatedConditions.name: Iridocyclitis (unspecified)
relatedConditions.relevance: Iridocyclitis (anterior uveitis) is the most common cause of hypopyon; recurrent iridocyclitis in the context of Behçet disease is the defining scenario for bilateral hypopyon.

relatedConditions.name: Bilateral panuveitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Bilateral hypopyon is often a feature of bilateral panuveitis, particularly in Behçet disease where all uveal layers are affected simultaneously.

relatedConditions.name: Glaucoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Secondary glaucoma from trabecular blockage or synechiae is a direct complication of untreated or recurrent hypopyon.

relatedConditions.name: Cataracts
relatedConditions.relevance: Prolonged intraocular inflammation and corticosteroid therapy used to treat hypopyon both accelerate posterior subcapsular cataract formation.

relatedConditions.name: Diabetic retinopathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Diabetes mellitus increases susceptibility to endogenous endophthalmitis via haematogenous spread, and diabetic patients with bilateral hypopyon require ophthalmic assessment for concurrent retinopathy.

faqs.question: What is bilateral hypopyon?
faqs.answer: Bilateral hypopyon is a collection of white blood cells that settles as a visible white layer at the bottom of the anterior chamber in both eyes simultaneously. It signals severe intraocular inflammation or infection and is an ophthalmic emergency requiring same-day specialist assessment.

faqs.question: What causes hypopyon in both eyes at the same time?
faqs.answer: Bilateral hypopyon almost always has a systemic cause. The most common are Behçet disease, bloodstream fungal or bacterial infection seeding both eyes (endogenous endophthalmitis), or severe bilateral corneal ulceration. Unlike one-sided hypopyon, a local eye infection rarely affects both eyes simultaneously.

faqs.question: Is bilateral hypopyon an emergency?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bilateral hypopyon is an ophthalmic emergency. Same-day evaluation by an ophthalmologist is required because delays of even 24 hours can result in permanent vision loss from infection or uncontrolled vasculitis.

faqs.question: How is hypopyon treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends on the cause. Infectious hypopyon is treated with intravitreal antibiotic or antifungal injections directly into the eye, combined with systemic antimicrobials. Behçet-associated hypopyon is treated with high-dose corticosteroids and long-term immunosuppressants such as azathioprine or infliximab.

faqs.question: Can bilateral hypopyon cause blindness?
faqs.answer: Yes. Without prompt treatment, bilateral hypopyon can cause permanent bilateral blindness. In Behçet disease, repeated uncontrolled attacks destroy retinal tissue; in endophthalmitis, bacterial toxins can devastate the eye within 24-48 hours. Early treatment dramatically improves outcomes.

faqs.question: What is the link between Behçet disease and hypopyon?
faqs.answer: Behçet disease causes recurrent attacks of sudden-onset panuveitis that characteristically produce a hypopyon — a white fluid level in the anterior chamber. It is one of the most recognized causes of bilateral hypopyon uveitis, particularly in patients from Turkey, the Middle East, and East Asia.

faqs.question: How is hypopyon different from a corneal ulcer?
faqs.answer: A corneal ulcer is an open sore on the cornea surface, while hypopyon is white blood cells that have settled inside the eye, in the anterior chamber below the cornea. Severe corneal ulcers can cause a secondary hypopyon through inflammation spreading inward, but they are separate pathologies.

faqs.question: What is endogenous endophthalmitis?
faqs.answer: Endogenous endophthalmitis is infection inside the eye caused by bacteria or fungi travelling through the bloodstream from a distant source such as a heart valve infection, a liver abscess, or an infected IV catheter. It can affect both eyes and produce bilateral hypopyon in severely ill or immunocompromised patients.

faqs.question: What eye drops are used for hypopyon?
faqs.answer: Topical corticosteroid drops (prednisolone acetate 1%) are applied frequently to reduce anterior chamber inflammation. Cycloplegic drops (atropine or cyclopentolate) relieve ciliary spasm and prevent synechiae. Antibiotic drops alone are insufficient for hypopyon caused by intraocular infection.

faqs.question: What is a vitreous tap and why might it be needed?
faqs.answer: A vitreous tap is a needle aspiration of fluid from inside the eye to obtain a sample for culture and microscopy. It is performed when endophthalmitis is suspected, allowing the pathogen to be identified so treatment can be targeted. Intravitreal antibiotics are injected at the same procedure.

faqs.question: Can contact lenses cause bilateral hypopyon?
faqs.answer: Extended-wear contact lenses can cause microbial keratitis in each eye if both lenses are contaminated or if the patient sleeps in lenses regularly. Each corneal ulcer can produce a secondary hypopyon in its respective eye, resulting in bilateral hypopyon through two independent local processes rather than a systemic cause.

faqs.question: What is a pseudo-hypopyon?
faqs.answer: A pseudo-hypopyon is a fluid level in the anterior chamber that looks like a hypopyon but contains cells other than white blood cells — for example, red blood cells from bleeding (layered haemorrhage), silicone oil from retinal surgery, or malignant cells from intraocular lymphoma. Distinguishing pseudo-hypopyon from true hypopyon alters management completely.

faqs.question: How long does it take for hypopyon to resolve with treatment?
faqs.answer: In Behçet uveitis treated with corticosteroids, the hypopyon typically resolves within 3-7 days. In infectious endophthalmitis treated with intravitreal antibiotics, improvement is seen in 24-72 hours. The underlying inflammation may take several weeks to fully settle.

faqs.question: Is bilateral hypopyon contagious?
faqs.answer: No. Hypopyon itself is not contagious. The inflammation or infection causing it may involve organisms that can be transmitted in other ways, but the eye condition cannot be spread from person to person.

faqs.question: What tests are done to find the cause of bilateral hypopyon?
faqs.answer: Tests include slit-lamp examination, blood cultures, blood glucose, serum fungal markers, CT scanning of the chest and abdomen, anterior chamber paracentesis for culture and cytology, and intraocular pressure measurement. In suspected Behçet disease, a clinical assessment using the International Criteria for Behçet's Disease is applied.

faqs.question: Can bilateral hypopyon come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes, particularly in Behçet disease, where recurrent attacks are the norm without long-term immunosuppression. Each recurrence carries a risk of cumulative vision loss. Maintenance therapy with azathioprine or anti-TNF biologics is continued indefinitely to prevent further episodes.

faqs.question: What is pars plana vitrectomy and when is it used for hypopyon?
faqs.answer: Pars plana vitrectomy is a surgical procedure that removes the vitreous gel from inside the eye through tiny incisions, reducing the load of infectious organisms or inflammatory cells and allowing sampling for diagnosis. It is used when hypopyon is caused by fungal endophthalmitis or when medical treatment alone is insufficient.

faqs.question: Does high blood sugar increase the risk of bilateral hypopyon?
faqs.answer: Yes. Uncontrolled diabetes impairs the immune response and predisposes to certain infections — particularly Klebsiella liver abscess — that can seed the eyes through the bloodstream. Diabetic patients with fever and eye symptoms should have an urgent ophthalmologic examination.

faqs.question: What is the difference between anterior uveitis and hypopyon?
faqs.answer: Anterior uveitis is inflammation of the uveal tract at the front of the eye, causing cells and flare in the aqueous humour. Hypopyon forms when the inflammatory cell density becomes so high that white cells settle under gravity to form a visible horizontal layer. Hypopyon represents severe anterior uveitis with a very heavy inflammatory burden.

faqs.question: Can children get bilateral hypopyon?
faqs.answer: Yes. In children, Behçet disease is rare but documented, and bilateral hypopyon can occur in this age group in endemic regions. Juvenile idiopathic arthritis-associated uveitis more commonly causes quiet chronic uveitis but can occasionally produce a hypopyon. Any child with a white fluid level in one or both eyes needs urgent ophthalmic assessment.

metaTitle: Bilateral Hypopyon: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Bilateral hypopyon is a white pus layer in both eyes signalling severe infection or Behçet disease. Learn causes, emergency signs, and treatment. Doctor-reviewed.
